metadata.title: Find All Duplicates in an Array

learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Loops, Index Manipulation, Time Complexity, Space Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine students are assigned locker numbers from 1 to n. Every student should occupy exactly one locker. If a locker is visited twice while marking attendance, that locker number represents a duplicate student ID.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine students are assigned locker numbers from 1 to n. Every student should occupy exactly one locker. If a locker is visited twice while marking attendance, that locker number represents a duplicate student ID.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use the Index Marking pattern whenever array values are limited to the range [1, n] and extra space is restricted.
learning.signals: Whenever values are guaranteed to lie in the range [1,n], think of using the array indices themselves to store information.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums of length n where every integer is in the range [1, n] and appears once or twice, return an array of all the integers that appear exactly twice. The algorithm must run in O(n) time and use only constant extra space (excluding the output array).
constraints: Each element appears once or twice.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Compare every pair of elements and collect duplicate values.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Since every value lies between 1 and n, each number has a unique corresponding index. Use that index to mark whether the number has already been seen. If the position is already marked, the number is a duplicate.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Every value has its own unique index because values lie in [1,n].
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever values are guaranteed to lie in the range [1,n], think of using the array indices themselves to store information.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Each element is processed exactly once. The input array itself stores visited information, eliminating the need for extra memory.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever values are guaranteed to lie in the range [1,n], think of using the array indices themselves to store information.
commonWrongPaths: Forgetting to use abs() after modifying values.
Using value instead of value-1 as the index.

hints: Every number maps to one index.
Use the array itself for marking.
Negative values can indicate visited positions.
commonMisconceptions: Forgetting to use abs() after modifying values.
Using value instead of value-1 as the index.

pattern.name: Index Marking
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever values are guaranteed to lie in the range [1,n], think of using the array indices themselves to store information.

approaches.title: Nested Loop Comparison
approaches.idea: Compare every pair of elements and collect duplicate values.

code.java: Brute force nested-loop solution omitted because it is inefficient.

approaches.title: HashSet
approaches.idea: Store visited numbers in a HashSet. Whenever a number is already present, add it to the answer.
algorithm: If current number exists, add to answer.

approaches.title: Index Marking (Negative Marking)
approaches.idea: Since values are in the range [1,n], each value maps to an index. Traverse the array, convert the corresponding index to negative to indicate it has been visited. If the position is already negative, the current value is a duplicate.
approaches.intuition: Each element is processed exactly once. The input array itself stores visited information, eliminating the need for extra memory.
algorithm: If nums[index] is negative, add abs(nums[i]) to answer.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[4,3,2,7,8,2,3,1]
Visit 4 → Mark index3
Visit 3 → Mark index2
Visit 2 → Mark index1
Visit 2 again → index1 already negative → Duplicate 2
Visit 3 again → Duplicate 3

visualization.description: Each number marks its own index.
steps: If already negative, x is a duplicate.

interviewFollowUps: Can you solve this without modifying the input array?
What if numbers appear three times?
How would you find missing numbers instead?
How is this related to Cyclic Sort?
Can you solve it using swapping?
interviewTips: Explain why the input constraints enable index marking.
Remember to use abs() after values become negative.
State O(n) time and O(1) extra space.
Mention Cyclic Sort as another valid approach.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Every value has its own unique index because values lie in [1,n].
cheatSheet.algorithm: Index = abs(value)-1 → If negative, duplicate → Else mark negative.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to use abs() after modifying values.
Using value instead of value-1 as the index.

youtube: NeetCode - Find All Duplicates in an Array
